nameMeanings.name: Hammond
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: English (of Norman origin): from the Middle English, Old French personal name Ha(i)mon, the oblique case form of the ancient Germanic Ha(i)mo, a short form of various compound names beginning with haim ‘home’. It frequently developed excrescent -d, giving Hamond, Haimund, and Hawmond. Alternatively, the name could derive from the Middle English personal name Hamund (Old Norse Hámundr, composed of the elements hár ‘high’ + mund ‘protection’), which may have been used in Normandy and in 12th-century eastern England, but the former explanation is more likely. The surname was sometimes confused with Almond and Ammon .

English: in the Bradford area of Yorkshire, the name is a shortened form of Ormondroyd, formerly Hamondesrode, from a lost place in Birstall (Yorkshire), named with the Middle English (Old French) personal name Hamon (1 above) + Middle English roid, a southern Yorkshire pronunciation of Old English rod ‘clearing’.
Irish: generally an importation from England, but occasionally an adopted name for Mac Ámoinn, see McCammon .

variants.name: Hammonds
meanings: English (Shropshire and Staffordshire): variant of Hammond , with post-medieval excrescent -s.

variants.name: Almond
meanings: from the Middle English personal name Almund, in which Old English Æthelmund ‘noble protector’ and Ealhmund ‘temple protector’ have coalesced.
variant of   Allman   and   Allmand   both meaning ‘German’, assimilated by folk etymology to the vocabulary word denoting the tree.

variants.name: Hammett
meanings: English (southwestern England, especialy Devon): from the Middle English personal name Hamet, a pet form of ancient Germanic Hamo (see Hammond 1).

variants.name: Hamon
meanings: Breton, French, and English (Channel Islands): from the Breton, Old French, Middle English personal name Hamon, the oblique case form of the ancient Germanic Ha(i)mo, a short form of various compound names beginning with haim ‘home’. See also Hammond .

variants.name: Hamlin
meanings: English and Irish: of Norman origin, from the Norman French and Middle English personal name Hamelin, a double diminutive of Hamo (see Hammond ).
French: variant of   Hamelin   and, in North America, (also) an altered form of this.

variants.name: Hampson
meanings: English (mainly Lancashire): patronymic from the Norman personal name Hamo, Hamon (see Hammond ).
Irish: shortened Anglicized form of Gaelic Ó hAmhsaigh ‘descendant of Amhsach’ a byname meaning ‘mercenary soldier’ or ‘messenger’, from the adjective amhasach ‘aggressive’.

variants.name: Haimes
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Jewish Yacov, Yosef.

English: variant, with genitival or post-medieval excrescent -s, of Haime, of Norman origin, from the Old French personal name Haim (ancient Germanic Haimo, from haim- ‘home’), often Latinized as Hamo. Compare Hammond .
Americanized form of some similar (like-sounding) Jewish surname.

variants.name: Hammon
meanings: English (Middlesex and Ken): variant of   Hammond  .

nameMeanings.name: William
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: Probably the most successful of all the Old French names of Germanic origin that were introduced to England by the Normans. It is derived from Germanic wil ‘will, desire’ + helm ‘helmet, protection’. The fact that it was borne by the Conqueror himself does not seem to have inhibited its favour with the ‘conquered’ population: in the first century after the Conquest it was the commonest male name of all, and not only among the Normans. In the later Middle Ages it was overtaken by John , but continued to run second to that name until the 20th century, when the picture became more fragmented.

historicEvents.title: Act of Uniformity
historicEvents.description: The Act of Uniformity was passed by the Parliament of England and required all people to go to church once a week. The consequence of not attending church was a fine of 12 pence, which was a considerable amount for a poor person.

historicEvents.title: State Lottery
historicEvents.description: A State Lottery was recorded in 1569. The tickets were sold at St. Paul's Cathedral in London.

historicEvents.title: The Royal Exchange is Organized
historicEvents.description: The Royal Exchange was organized in January 1571 by Sir Thomas Gresham. Gresham is known as the father of English banking.

en.lifeSummary: William Hammond Sr was born about 1555, in Slimbridge, Gloucestershire, England. He had at least 1 son. He died about 1608, in his hometown, at the age of 55.

fr.lifeSummary: William Hammond Sr est né environ 1555, à Slimbridge, Gloucestershire, Angleterre. Il a eu au moins 1 fils. Il est décédé environ 1608, dans sa ville natale, à l’âge de 55 ans.

de.lifeSummary: William Hammond Sr wurde um 1555 in Slimbridge, Gloucestershire, England geboren. Er hatte mindestens 1 Sohn. Er starb um 1608 im Alter von 55 Jahren in seinem Geburtsort.

it.lifeSummary: William Hammond Sr è nato il circa 1555 nel Slimbridge, Gloucestershire, England. Lui avuto almeno 1 figlio. Morì circa 1608, nel sua cittadina, all'età di 55 anni.

ko.lifeSummary: William Hammond Sr(은)는 대략 1555년 영국 그루스스터실르 슬림브리지에서 태어났다. 그는 적어도 한 명의 아들을 두었다. 그는 대략 1608년 그의 고향에서 55세의 나이로 사망했다.

pt.lifeSummary: William Hammond Sr nasceu aproximadamente 1555, em Slimbridge, Gloucestershire, Inglaterra. Ele teve pelo menos 1 filho. Ele faleceu aproximadamente 1608, em sua cidade natal, com 55 anos.

ru.lifeSummary: William Hammond Sr родился около 1555 г. в местности слимбридж, глоусестиршир, Англия. У него был по крайней мере 1 сын. Он умер около 1608 г. в его родном городе в возрасте 55 лет.

es.lifeSummary: William Hammond Sr nació aproximadamente 1555, en Slimbridge, Gloucestershire, Inglaterra. Tuvo por lo menos 1 hijo. Murió aproximadamente 1608, en su pueblo, a la edad de 55 años.
